worked: `launchctl kickstart -k` as the right primitive when a process is supervised: atomic kill+restart inside the supervisor's view, no race window. The general principle ("when a supervisor is in play, don't bypass it") is worth carrying forward.
The `actions.sh <cmd-id> <args-json>` two-argv contract is the cheapest possible plugin-args interface — host enforces the envelope shape, plugin parses whatever keys it needs via jq.

Splitting changes into three bisectable commits (`bca0a49`, `24f9a7a`, `efb76d0`) preserved the bisect signal: if row-click dispatch regresses you land on the host-plumbing commit, not on a mashed-together "everything" commit.
Confirm-gating destructive ops via NSAlert with Cancel pre-selected for destructive=true is a low-cost safeguard worth the boilerplate.
didnt_work: Interpreting "keep going" as a directive to ship the next pre-planned phase, when the user's most recent affect was frustration. The continuation directive carried the affect of the prior turn — and the prior turn was "still useless". I should have stopped to ask one grounding question ("when you sit down at the menubar and click this, what task are you trying to accomplish?") before queueing the next infrastructure layer.

Framing each batch as "this fixes it." That framing implicitly admits the prior ship was the problem, then promises a structural fix from an additive layer. If the prior ship didn't address the gap, more surface area is unlikely to either.

The architectural premise itself may have been wrong. The user originally asked for three concrete things — manage Claude-created crons, inspect hooks, browse atone assets — and the V2 build wrapped them in a generic plugin platform. Plugin framework felt "professional" but is probably overkill for a single-author single-user macOS menubar tool. This is the dream-pin question.
gotchas: **Parallel Edit calls to the same file silently clobber each other.** Two consecutive Edit calls on `plugins/sessions/fetch.sh` in one tool batch both returned "updated successfully" but only one (or neither) actually persisted — file timestamp confirmed no change. Workaround: serialize Edits to the same file. Filing this is worth considering as a separate atone pattern; it bit twice this session.
**`i-dream pin add --from-json` via heredoc embedded a control char** that broke JSON parsing. Workaround: write the JSON to a tempfile, pipe stdin from the file.
**macOS 13 target precludes the two-param `onChange(of:_:)` overload** (requires macOS 14). Use the deprecated one-param form for now.

**`crontab -e` is non-interactive-hostile.** Toggling cron lines requires reading the full crontab, rewriting in memory, and piping the new version to `crontab -`. The exact-line-match strategy (vs line-number) is concurrency-safe but means refresh-before-toggle is critical.
**The `--from-json` PinEvent CLI was strict** about control chars but lenient about field types — `text` is freeform string, no length cap apparent in the schema.
notes: **V2 is shelved by user request.** Do not pick V2 work back up without an explicit fresh ask. If the user invokes `/catchup` against this checkpoint, the right move is to acknowledge the shutdown state and ask what they want to do next — NOT to suggest resuming V2 plugin development.
All 5 commits on the `v2` branch are intact; nothing was reverted. The branch is in a structurally-functional state — if/when V2 work resumes, the wiring done this session is reusable.
